content:
# Joshua Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 237-238 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_014_page_001.jpg`

---

*[Caleb Claims His Mountain — Fifteen verses that pivot from bureaucratic land-division to one old warrior's magnificent speech. The chapter opens with dry procedural notes about how the land west of the Jordan was distributed, then suddenly Caleb son of Jephunneh steps forward and delivers one of the most stirring personal testimonies in the Hebrew Bible. He is eighty-five years old, as strong as the day Moses sent him to spy out the land forty-five years ago, and he wants the hardest territory — the hill country where the Anakim giants still live. This is a man who has waited half a lifetime for a promise and has not grown tired of believing. PNEUMA lets Caleb's voice ring with the vigor the Hebrew gives him.]*

---

**1** Here is what the Sons of Israel received as their inheritance in the land of Canaan — the territory that Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the ancestral tribes distributed to them. **2** Their inheritance was assigned by lot, as YHWH had commanded through Moses, for the nine-and-a-half tribes. **3** Moses had already given the two-and-a-half tribes their inheritance east of the Jordan, but to the Levites he had given no land inheritance among them. **4** This was because the descendants of Joseph had become two tribes — Manasseh and Ephraim — and the Levites were given no territorial share in the land, only cities to live in along with pasturelands for their herds and flocks.

**5** The Sons of Israel carried out the distribution just as YHWH had commanded Moses — and so they divided the land.

**6** Now the people of Judah came to Joshua at Gilgal, and Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite said to him: "You know what YHWH said to Moses the man of God about you and me at Kadesh-Barnea. **7** I was forty years old when Moses, the servant of YHWH, sent me from Kadesh-Barnea to scout out the land, and I brought back an honest report — what I truly believed. **8** My fellow scouts who went with me made the people lose heart, but I gave myself completely to YHWH my God. **9** On that day Moses swore an oath: 'The land your feet have walked on will belong to you and your descendants forever, because you gave yourself completely to YHWH my God.'

**10** "And now — look! YHWH has kept me alive, just as he promised, these forty-five years since YHWH made that promise to Moses, all through the time Israel wandered in the wilderness. Here I am today, eighty-five years old! **11** I am still as strong today as I was the day Moses sent me out. My strength for battle, for marching out, and for marching home is the same as it was then. **12** So give me this hill country that YHWH promised me that day. You yourself heard then that the Anakim are there, with great fortified cities. But if YHWH is with me, I will drive them out, just as YHWH promised."

**13** Joshua blessed him, and gave Hebron to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his inheritance. **14** That is why Hebron has belonged to Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite to this day — because he gave himself completely to YHWH, the God of Israel.

**15** (Hebron was formerly called Kiriath-Arba — Arba being the greatest man among the Anakim.) And the land had rest from war.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1-5 — The procedural frame:** These verses are administrative scaffolding — explaining the mechanism of land distribution before the narrative proper begins. Three facts are established: (a) the allocation was by sacred lot, not human preference; (b) the Transjordanian tribes already had their land; (c) the Levites received no territory, which is why twelve tribal territories exist despite Levi's exclusion (Joseph's line split into two tribes to fill the gap). PNEUMA renders this section in clear expository prose, because that is what it is — the narrator organizing his material before the dramatic scene.

**V. 6 — "The Kenizzite" (haqqenizzi):** This ethnic label connects Caleb to the Kenizzite clan listed among pre-Israelite peoples in Genesis 15:19. It suggests Caleb's family was originally non-Israelite, adopted into the tribe of Judah. The narrator mentions it without apology. Caleb's devotion to YHWH is what defines him, not his bloodline. PNEUMA preserves the designation without explanatory gloss in the text.

**V. 7 — "An honest report — what I truly believed":** The Hebrew reads literally "I brought back word to him as was with my heart." The idiom ka'asher im-levavi means "according to what was in my heart" — that is, truthfully, without distortion. Caleb contrasts his honest report with the fear-driven exaggeration of the other spies. PNEUMA renders the meaning: an honest report that reflected his genuine conviction.

**V. 8 — "Gave myself completely" (mille' acharei):** This is the key phrase of the chapter, appearing three times (vv. 8, 9, 14). The Hebrew literally means "filled after" — the Piel of male' ("to fill") with the preposition acharei ("after"). It is an idiom for total, unreserved devotion — following without holding anything back. Most translations use "followed wholeheartedly" or "wholly followed." PNEUMA chooses "gave myself completely to" because in dynamic English, the sense is one of total self-commitment, not merely walking behind someone. The filling metaphor suggests a life so devoted that no space remains for competing loyalties.

**V. 8 — "Made the people lose heart" (himsu et-levav ha'am):** Literally "melted the heart of the people." The Hiphil of masas means to cause something solid to dissolve — the same verb used to describe Canaanite terror in Joshua 2:11 and 5:1. Here the irony is sharp: Israel's own scouts did to Israel's courage what Israel was supposed to do to the Canaanites. PNEUMA renders "lose heart" as the natural English equivalent of courage dissolving.

**V. 10 — "Here I am today, eighty-five years old!":** The Hebrew uses the age formula ben-chamesh ushemonim shanah ("a son of eighty-five years"). Caleb's arithmetic is significant: he was forty at Kadesh-Barnea, thirty-eight more years in the wilderness, and approximately seven years of conquest — totaling eighty-five. The double hinneh ("behold") in the Hebrew of v. 10 conveys Caleb's astonishment and gratitude that he is still alive and still strong. PNEUMA captures this with "And now — look!" and "Here I am today."

**V. 11 — "My strength for battle, for marching out, and for marching home":** The Hebrew triad is lammilchamah ("for war"), v'latze't ("and to go out"), v'lavo' ("and to come in"). "Going out and coming in" is a standard Hebrew merism for all of life's activities, especially military campaigns (cf. Deuteronomy 31:2, 1 Kings 3:7). Caleb is not merely boasting about fitness; he is declaring himself combat-ready for the specific mission of taking the hill country. PNEUMA expands the merism slightly for clarity.

**V. 12 — "If YHWH is with me" (ulai):** The Hebrew particle ulai means "perhaps" — and its use here is remarkable. Caleb has just recounted YHWH's promise, yet he speaks with reverent contingency: "perhaps YHWH will be with me." This is not doubt. It is the piety of a man who knows that YHWH's promises are sure but that YHWH's sovereign freedom must be honored. PNEUMA renders "if YHWH is with me" to convey the conditional deference in natural English, though the Hebrew is even more tentative ("perhaps").

**V. 12 — The Anakim:** The Anakim (or "sons of Anak") were the legendary giant inhabitants of Canaan who terrified the original spies (Numbers 13:28, 33). The ten scouts saw them and said, "We seemed like grasshoppers in our own eyes." Caleb saw the same giants and said, "Give me that mountain." Forty-five years later, the giants are still there, and Caleb still wants the fight. The narrative power of this request depends on remembering Numbers 13-14: Caleb is asking for the very territory that caused all of Israel to rebel in fear.

**V. 13 — "Joshua blessed him":** The verb vayvarekheihu ("and he blessed him") is more than a verbal benediction. In this context it is the formal authorization of a land grant — Joshua's official bestowal of Hebron upon Caleb. The blessing ratifies the promise Moses made forty-five years earlier.

**V. 14 — "To this day":** The etiological formula 'ad ha-yom ha-zeh ("until this day") is the narrator's voice breaking through, speaking from a later era when Caleb's descendants still held Hebron. It is the historian's footnote: this is not just ancient history — it shaped the landscape we still see.

**V. 15 — "Kiriath-Arba":** The ancient name of Hebron, meaning "City of Arba." The clause identifying Arba as "the greatest man among the Anakim" is an antiquarian note — the narrator explaining an old place name. Hebron is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world and carries enormous patriarchal significance: Abraham bought the cave of Machpelah there to bury Sarah (Genesis 23). Caleb's inheritance thus connects him to the deepest roots of Israel's story.

**V. 15 — "The land had rest from war":** This closing formula (v'ha'aretz shaqetah milchamah) is a structural marker that appears at key transition points in Joshua (cf. 11:23). It signals the end of a narrative unit. The verb shaqat means to be quiet, undisturbed, at peace — the land itself is the subject. After all the conquest narratives, the land finally exhales.

**On Caleb's speech as rhetoric:** Caleb's address in vv. 6-12 is a masterpiece of persuasion. He establishes his credentials (v. 7), contrasts himself with the faithless spies (v. 8), cites the divine oath (v. 9), demonstrates that YHWH has kept his end of the bargain (v. 10), proves his fitness for the task (v. 11), and then — only then — makes his request (v. 12). He asks for the hardest land, not the easiest. The speech moves from past faithfulness to present strength to future trust. It is the voice of a man who has spent forty-five years waiting without growing bitter, and who wants one more impossible thing from God.

— PNEUMA
